guest_role: Founder and CEO, Guru (previously founder of Boomi, acquired by Dell)
assertiveness_why: Harry asks thoughtful, prepared multi-part questions and probes for specifics ('double clicking on that'), but he never contests or challenges any of Rick's claims, staying in pure-reception mode throughout.
question_style: Open-ended and empathy-driven questions sourced from named investors (Ryan at Thrive, Jake at Emergence), with genuine personal follow-ups (on burnout, vulnerability, firing people) but no pushback or planted counter-thesis; Harry shares his own struggles (conflict-aversion) to invite candor rather than to challenge.
tone: Warm, personal, and vulnerable-forward; visibly fannish about the guest's culture and leadership style, with self-deprecating admissions (conflict-averse, hasn't read enough).
depth: Mostly leadership/culture/organizational depth (values reinforcement mechanics, internal promotion vs external hiring, remote/non-hub geography strategy) rather than product or technical depth.
harry_quotes: What didn't work at Boomi that you're maybe not doing at Guru?
Have you ever been burnt out?
How do you feel with those moments, and what would you advise me?
learnings: Company values should be revisited and rewritten roughly annually rather than treated as permanent, since what a company needs evolves with its stage.
A structured culture-fit interview conducted by someone outside the hiring team, with veto power, can protect culture even as hiring scales.
Firing conversations land more fairly when preceded by a documented performance-improvement conversation, so the outcome doesn't feel like a surprise.
Building outside a core tech hub can be a hiring advantage because it avoids talent-war saturation and echo-chamber groupthink common in oversaturated hubs.
Investors are increasingly indifferent to (or even prefer) portfolio companies based outside Silicon Valley/New York, a marked shift from a decade earlier.
Internal promotion works best when paired with clear autonomy -- giving people stretch responsibility as they show initiative, rather than gatekeeping advancement.
repeat_guest_callback: So I'm so pleased you mentioned Anna. I've actually had her on the show before, and she is just fantastic.
notable: Ends on an unusually personal quickfire segment covering burnout and coping mechanisms rather than the standard investor/tactical questions.
